planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown palms in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Canary Island date palm is tolerant of salt spray and coastal conditions. It tolerates light frost to about 15-18°F when established but young plants need protection below 25°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Canary Island date palm is the most majestic and widely planted ornamental palm in the world — a massive, slow-growing palm with an immense crown of arching, dark green, pinnate fronds each up to 18 feet long, creating a fountain-like canopy of extraordinary scale and beauty. The pineapple-like patterned trunk develops from years of old frond bases and becomes a powerful architectural statement in landscapes. Native to the Canary Islands, it has been planted throughout the Mediterranean, California, Florida, and worldwide tropical and subtropical climates. The orange-yellow fruits produced in massive pendant clusters are date-like and technically edible but too astringent for pleasant eating. Mature trees create the grandest scale of any landscape palm — worth every inch of space. Widely used as street trees in California, Spain, Portugal, and Florida. Fusarium wilt disease (Fusarium oxysporum) is a serious and fatal threat; purchase only certified disease-free specimens.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial shade; kentia palm is the most tolerant of all palms of indoor low-light conditions and is the premier interior landscape palm. Outdoors it prefers shade or dappled light and moist, fertile, well-drained conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial to full shade; kentia palm is the world's most popular indoor palm and one of the most important ornamental plants in the global interior plant industry — exported from its tiny native range (Lord Howe Island, Australia) in the form of seeds and seedlings for over 150 years to supply interior landscape plantings worldwide. The graceful, arching, dark green pinnate fronds and the extremely high tolerance of low light, low humidity, and neglect make it the standard palm for hotel lobbies, office buildings, and living rooms. Outdoors in zones 9–11, kentia palm is an attractive garden palm for shaded or semi-shaded positions under larger trees, unusually shade-tolerant for a palm. The seed germination is notoriously slow and erratic (months to a year). One of the few palms genuinely adapted to deep shade — a valuable characteristic that distinguishes it from almost all other palms.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown palms in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile to average, moist to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; king palm grows rapidly in warm, humid conditions and is one of the faster-growing palms for subtropical landscapes. Frost-sensitive — protect young plants below 28°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile to average, moist to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; king palm is one of the most attractive and widely planted palms in subtropical and warm-temperate regions worldwide — a graceful, fast-growing palm with a distinctive, smooth, gray-green trunk, a bright green crownshaft (the trunk extension formed by the tightly overlapping leaf bases), and a full crown of arching, bright green pinnate fronds. Native to the subtropical rainforest margins of coastal Queensland and New South Wales, Australia, it grows naturally in semi-shade along stream banks. The clusters of bright red fruits produced beneath the crownshaft are ornamental. More cold-sensitive than California fan palm or Canary Island palm. Widely used as a street, avenue, and specimen palm in California, Florida, Hawaii, Australia, and the Mediterranean. Grows in partial shade — unusual tolerance for a large, pinnate-leaf palm.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; black bamboo is a running bamboo (spreading rhizomes) that requires a root barrier (18-inch deep metal or HDPE sheet) to prevent invasive spread in garden settings.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; ALWAYS install a bamboo root barrier (18-inch deep polypropylene or metal sheet) before planting black bamboo in garden settings — it is a running bamboo with spreading rhizomes that will aggressively colonize surrounding areas without physical containment. The culms (canes) emerge green and gradually turn jet black over 2–3 years as they age — the dramatic contrast between the feathery green leaves and the polished, ebony-black canes is uniquely striking and one of the most beautiful ornamental effects of any bamboo. Widely used in Chinese gardens, Japanese-inspired gardens, and contemporary garden design. The young canes (bamboo shoots) are edible in spring when they first emerge, with a mild flavor. Used decoratively in flower arrangements and for making chopsticks, fans, and bamboo crafts. Container growing in a large barrel eliminates spread concerns.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; moso bamboo is a running bamboo requiring a bamboo root barrier (24-inch deep minimum for this large species) to prevent aggressive spreading. A single established grove can produce hundreds of edible shoots annually.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained loamy soil in full sun; moso bamboo is the most economically important bamboo in the world — the source of most commercial bamboo timber, flooring, furniture, textiles, and paper produced globally. It also produces the most prized bamboo shoots in Chinese cuisine, harvested in spring when they emerge from the ground. The new shoots can be harvested before they emerge from the soil (the pale, tender, underground form) or just as they break the surface. ALWAYS install a deep root barrier (24 inches minimum) before planting — moso is an aggressive running bamboo that can devastate surrounding plantings and structures without physical containment. The towering, green culms with their distinctive white waxy powder on new growth reach 40–90 feet tall, creating a dramatic grove effect reminiscent of Chinese mountain paintings. Once established, a well-managed grove produces an annual harvest of both timber culms and edible shoots.
